what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Basic understanding of OOP, classes, objects, and real-world modeling.
sample_strong_answer: Object-Oriented Programming is a programming approach that organizes code using objects and classes. It helps in reusability, modularity, and better code organization.
common_mistakes_to_avoid: No mention of classes and objects
follow_up_questions: What are the main pillars of OOP?
Can you give a real-world example of OOP?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Definition of class as a blueprint and its role in creating objects.
sample_strong_answer: A class is a blueprint or template used to create objects. It defines properties and methods that objects of that class can use.
follow_up_questions: How is a class different from an object?
Can you write a simple class example?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Understanding object as an instance of a class.
sample_strong_answer: An object is an instance of a class. It has data and behavior defined by the class.
follow_up_questions: Can one class create multiple objects?
What is object state and behavior?

question_text: What are the four pillars of OOP?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Knowledge of encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction.
sample_strong_answer: The four pillars of OOP are encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction. They help in secure, reusable, flexible, and simplified software design.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Data hiding and binding data with methods.
sample_strong_answer: Encapsulation is the process of binding data and methods together and restricting direct access to data using access modifiers or private variables.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Understanding parent-child class relationship and code reuse.
sample_strong_answer: Inheritance allows one class to acquire properties and methods of another class. It promotes code reuse and hierarchy.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Understanding one name, many forms; compile-time and runtime.
sample_strong_answer: Polymorphism means one function or method can behave differently based on context. It can be achieved through method overloading and method overriding.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Hiding internal implementation and showing essential details.
sample_strong_answer: Abstraction means showing only necessary details to the user and hiding internal implementation. It reduces complexity.
follow_up_questions: What is the difference between abstraction and encapsulation?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Same method name with different parameters.
sample_strong_answer: Method overloading means defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists in the same class.
common_mistakes_to_avoid: Saying same method with same parameters
follow_up_questions: Is method overloading compile-time or runtime polymorphism?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Child class redefining parent class method.
sample_strong_answer: Method overriding happens when a child class provides its own implementation of a method already defined in the parent class.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Special method used to initialize objects.
sample_strong_answer: A constructor is a special method that is automatically called when an object is created. It initializes object data.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Object cleanup and memory/resource release.
sample_strong_answer: A destructor is a special method used to clean up resources when an object is destroyed or no longer needed.

question_text: What is the difference between class variable and instance variable?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Class-level shared variable vs object-specific variable.
sample_strong_answer: A class variable is shared by all objects of a class, while an instance variable belongs to a specific object and can have different values for each object.
follow_up_questions: When do you use instance variables?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Public, private, protected access control.
sample_strong_answer: Access modifiers control visibility of class members. Common access modifiers are public, private, and protected.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Dependency between modules/classes.
sample_strong_answer: Coupling refers to the level of dependency between modules or classes. Low coupling is preferred because it makes code easier to maintain.
common_mistakes_to_avoid: Saying high coupling is always good

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: How closely related responsibilities of a module are.
sample_strong_answer: Cohesion means how strongly the responsibilities of a module or class are related. High cohesion is preferred for clean design.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Contract defining methods without full implementation.
sample_strong_answer: An interface defines a contract that classes can implement. It usually contains method declarations that implementing classes must define.
follow_up_questions: Difference between interface and abstract class?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Class that cannot be directly instantiated and may have abstract methods.
sample_strong_answer: An abstract class is a class that cannot be directly instantiated. It can contain abstract methods and concrete methods.
follow_up_questions: When do you use abstract class?
Can abstract class have concrete methods?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Handling runtime errors gracefully.
sample_strong_answer: Exception handling is a mechanism to handle runtime errors so that the program does not crash unexpectedly. It uses blocks like try, catch, finally, or except.

question_text: What is the difference between error and exception?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Serious system-level problem vs handleable runtime issue.
sample_strong_answer: An error is usually a serious problem that may not be recoverable, while an exception is a runtime issue that can be handled using exception handling.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Definition, purpose, and data management role.
sample_strong_answer: DBMS stands for Database Management System. It is software used to store, manage, retrieve, and organize data efficiently.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Relational database and table-based storage.
sample_strong_answer: RDBMS stands for Relational Database Management System. It stores data in tables with rows and columns and supports relationships between tables.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Organized collection of data.
sample_strong_answer: A database is an organized collection of data that can be stored, accessed, and managed easily.
follow_up_questions: What is the difference between database and DBMS?

question_text: What is a table in DBMS?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Rows and columns structure.
sample_strong_answer: A table is a database object that stores data in rows and columns. Each row represents a record and each column represents an attribute.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Unique identification of rows.
sample_strong_answer: A primary key is a column or set of columns that uniquely identifies each record in a table. It cannot be null and must be unique.
common_mistakes_to_avoid: Saying primary key can be null
follow_up_questions: Can a table have multiple primary keys?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Key used to connect two tables.
sample_strong_answer: A foreign key is a column in one table that refers to the primary key of another table. It is used to maintain relationships between tables.
follow_up_questions: Can foreign key have duplicate values?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Columns capable of uniquely identifying records.
sample_strong_answer: A candidate key is any column or combination of columns that can uniquely identify a row in a table. One candidate key is selected as the primary key.
follow_up_questions: How is candidate key different from primary key?
Can a table have multiple candidate keys?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Combination of columns used as key.
sample_strong_answer: A composite key is a key made using two or more columns to uniquely identify a record in a table.
follow_up_questions: When do we use composite key?
Is composite key a primary key?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Organizing database to reduce redundancy and improve consistency.
sample_strong_answer: Normalization is the process of organizing data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity by dividing data into related tables.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Adding redundancy to improve read performance.
sample_strong_answer: Denormalization is the process of combining data or adding redundancy to improve read performance, usually at the cost of extra storage.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Atomic values and no repeating groups.
sample_strong_answer: First Normal Form means each column should contain atomic values and there should be no repeating groups.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: 1NF plus no partial dependency.
sample_strong_answer: Second Normal Form means the table is in 1NF and every non-key attribute fully depends on the complete primary key.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: 2NF plus no transitive dependency.
sample_strong_answer: Third Normal Form means the table is in 2NF and non-key attributes should not depend on other non-key attributes.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Query language for managing relational databases.
sample_strong_answer: SQL stands for Structured Query Language. It is used to create, retrieve, update, and delete data in relational databases.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Data Definition Language commands.
sample_strong_answer: DDL commands are used to define or modify database structure. Examples include CREATE, ALTER, DROP, and TRUNCATE.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Data Manipulation Language commands.
sample_strong_answer: DML commands are used to manipulate data stored in tables. Examples include INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and SELECT.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Data Control Language for permissions.
sample_strong_answer: DCL commands are used to control access permissions in a database. Examples include GRANT and REVOKE.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Transaction Control Language commands.
sample_strong_answer: TCL commands are used to manage transactions. Examples include COMMIT, ROLLBACK, and SAVEPOINT.

question_text: What is a JOIN in SQL?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Combining rows from multiple tables.
sample_strong_answer: A JOIN is used to combine data from two or more tables based on related columns.
follow_up_questions: Difference between inner join and left join?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Returns matching records from both tables.
sample_strong_answer: INNER JOIN returns only the records that have matching values in both tables.
follow_up_questions: When do we use inner join?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: All records from left table and matched records from right table.
sample_strong_answer: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. If no match exists, NULL values are returned for right table columns.
follow_up_questions: Difference between left join and right join?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Query inside another query.
sample_strong_answer: A subquery is a query written inside another SQL query. It is used to get intermediate results for the outer query.

question_text: What is an index in DBMS?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Data structure that improves search performance.
sample_strong_answer: An index is a database structure that improves query search speed by allowing faster data lookup, similar to an index in a book.
follow_up_questions: What is the disadvantage of indexes?

question_text: What is a view in SQL?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Virtual table based on query result.
sample_strong_answer: A view is a virtual table created from a SQL query. It does not usually store data physically but shows data from one or more tables.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Saved SQL code that can be reused.
sample_strong_answer: A stored procedure is a pre-written set of SQL statements stored in the database and executed when needed.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Transaction reliability: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.
sample_strong_answer: ACID properties ensure reliable database transactions. They stand for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Single logical unit of work.
sample_strong_answer: A transaction is a set of database operations performed as a single logical unit. It should either complete fully or fail completely.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Duplicate or repeated data.
sample_strong_answer: Data redundancy means unnecessary duplication of data in a database. It can cause storage waste and inconsistency.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Accuracy and consistency of data.
sample_strong_answer: Data integrity means maintaining accurate, consistent, and reliable data throughout its lifecycle.
follow_up_questions: What are examples of integrity constraints?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: OS as interface between user and hardware.
sample_strong_answer: An operating system is system software that manages hardware resources and provides services for application programs. It acts as an interface between user and hardware.

question_text: What are the main functions of an operating system?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Process, memory, file, device, and security management.
sample_strong_answer: Main functions of an OS include process management, memory management, file management, device management, and security.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Program in execution.
sample_strong_answer: A process is a program in execution. It has its own memory space, resources, and execution state.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Lightweight unit of execution within a process.
sample_strong_answer: A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads can share the same process resources.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Running multiple tasks apparently at same time.
sample_strong_answer: Multitasking is the ability of an operating system to run multiple tasks or processes at the same time by sharing CPU time.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Using multiple CPUs/processors for execution.
sample_strong_answer: Multiprocessing means using two or more processors or CPU cores to execute processes simultaneously.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: CPU switching from one process/thread to another.
sample_strong_answer: Context switching is the process of saving the state of a running process and loading the state of another process so the CPU can switch execution.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Selecting process for CPU execution.
sample_strong_answer: CPU scheduling is the method used by the operating system to decide which process gets the CPU next.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: First Come First Served scheduling.
sample_strong_answer: FCFS scheduling executes processes in the order they arrive. It is simple but can cause long waiting time.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Time quantum based scheduling.
sample_strong_answer: Round Robin scheduling gives each process a fixed time quantum. After the time expires, the process goes back to the ready queue if not completed.
follow_up_questions: What happens if time quantum is too small?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Processes waiting forever for resources.
sample_strong_answer: Deadlock is a situation where two or more processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other.

question_text: What are the necessary conditions for deadlock?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait.
sample_strong_answer: The four necessary conditions for deadlock are mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.
follow_up_questions: Which condition can be removed to prevent deadlock?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Managing main memory allocation and deallocation.
sample_strong_answer: Memory management is the OS function that manages allocation and deallocation of main memory to processes.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Using secondary storage as extension of RAM.
sample_strong_answer: Virtual memory is a memory management technique that uses disk space as an extension of RAM, allowing larger programs to run.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Dividing memory into fixed-size pages/frames.
sample_strong_answer: Paging is a memory management technique where logical memory is divided into pages and physical memory into frames.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Dividing memory into variable-sized logical segments.
sample_strong_answer: Segmentation divides memory into logical variable-sized segments such as code, data, and stack.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Requested page not present in main memory.
sample_strong_answer: A page fault occurs when a program tries to access a page that is not currently present in main memory.
follow_up_questions: How does OS handle page fault?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Method to store and organize files.
sample_strong_answer: A file system is a method used by the operating system to store, organize, retrieve, and manage files on storage devices.
follow_up_questions: Why do we need file systems?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Small fast memory near CPU.
sample_strong_answer: Cache memory is a small and fast memory located close to the CPU that stores frequently used data for faster access.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Interface for program to request OS services.
sample_strong_answer: A system call is a way for a program to request services from the operating system, such as file access or process creation.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Connected devices sharing data/resources.
sample_strong_answer: A computer network is a group of connected devices that can communicate and share data or resources.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Local Area Network in small area.
sample_strong_answer: LAN stands for Local Area Network. It connects devices within a small area such as a home, office, or college.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Wide Area Network across large geography.
sample_strong_answer: WAN stands for Wide Area Network. It connects networks across large geographical areas, such as cities or countries.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Seven-layer network reference model.
sample_strong_answer: The OSI model is a seven-layer reference model that explains how data communication happens in networks.

question_text: Name the seven layers of OSI model.
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Correct OSI layer order.
sample_strong_answer: The seven OSI layers are Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Practical internet protocol suite model.
sample_strong_answer: The TCP/IP model is a networking model used for internet communication. It includes layers such as network access, internet, transport, and application.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Unique logical address of device in network.
sample_strong_answer: An IP address is a unique logical address assigned to a device on a network to identify and communicate with it.
follow_up_questions: Difference between public and private IP?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Physical hardware address of network device.
sample_strong_answer: A MAC address is a unique hardware address assigned to a network interface card. It is used for communication within a local network.
follow_up_questions: Difference between MAC and IP address?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Domain name to IP address translation.
sample_strong_answer: DNS stands for Domain Name System. It translates domain names like google.com into IP addresses.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Protocol for web communication.
sample_strong_answer: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol. It is used for communication between web browsers and web servers.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Secure version of HTTP.
sample_strong_answer: HTTPS is the secure version of HTTP. It uses encryption to protect data transferred between browser and server.
common_mistakes_to_avoid: Saying it is same as HTTP

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Reliable connection-oriented transport protocol.
sample_strong_answer: TCP is a connection-oriented protocol that provides reliable data delivery with error checking and ordering.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Connectionless faster protocol.
sample_strong_answer: UDP is a connectionless protocol that sends data quickly but does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or error recovery.
follow_up_questions: Why is UDP faster than TCP?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Reliability, connection, speed, and use cases.
sample_strong_answer: TCP is reliable, connection-oriented, and slower, while UDP is connectionless, faster, and does not guarantee delivery.
follow_up_questions: Which protocol is used for video streaming?
Which is used for file transfer?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Device that forwards packets between networks.
sample_strong_answer: A router is a networking device that forwards data packets between different networks and helps connect devices to the internet.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Device connecting devices in LAN.
sample_strong_answer: A switch connects multiple devices in a local network and forwards data to the correct device using MAC addresses.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Security system controlling network traffic.
sample_strong_answer: A firewall is a network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on security rules.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Maximum data transfer capacity.
sample_strong_answer: Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network in a given time.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Delay in data transmission.
sample_strong_answer: Latency is the time delay between sending a request and receiving a response over a network.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Data divided into packets for transmission.
sample_strong_answer: Packet switching is a method where data is divided into small packets and sent independently across a network.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Way to organize and store data.
sample_strong_answer: A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data so it can be accessed and modified efficiently.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Collection of same type elements stored contiguously.
sample_strong_answer: An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations and accessed using index positions.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Linear structure with nodes connected by pointers.
sample_strong_answer: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, and each node points to the next node.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: LIFO data structure.
sample_strong_answer: A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO, meaning Last In First Out. Insert and delete operations happen at the top.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: FIFO data structure.
sample_strong_answer: A queue is a linear data structure that follows FIFO, meaning First In First Out. Insertion happens at rear and deletion at front.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Hierarchical data structure.
sample_strong_answer: A tree is a hierarchical data structure made of nodes, with one root node and child nodes.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Tree where each node has at most two children.
sample_strong_answer: A binary tree is a tree data structure where each node can have at most two children, usually called left and right child.

question_text: What is a binary search tree?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Binary tree with ordered left and right subtrees.
sample_strong_answer: A binary search tree is a binary tree where the left child has smaller values and the right child has greater values than the root.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Nodes and edges data structure.
sample_strong_answer: A graph is a data structure made of vertices and edges, used to represent relationships between objects.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Mapping keys to values using hash function.
sample_strong_answer: Hashing is a technique that uses a hash function to map keys to indexes for fast data storage and retrieval.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: How algorithm running time grows with input size.
sample_strong_answer: Time complexity describes how the running time of an algorithm increases as the input size grows.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Memory used by an algorithm based on input size.
sample_strong_answer: Space complexity describes how much memory an algorithm uses as input size increases.
follow_up_questions: Can time and space be traded off?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Worst-case growth representation.
sample_strong_answer: Big O notation is used to represent the growth rate of an algorithm's time or space complexity, usually in worst-case terms.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Searching elements one by one.
sample_strong_answer: Linear search checks each element one by one until the target element is found or the list ends.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Search algorithm on sorted data by dividing range.
sample_strong_answer: Binary search is an efficient searching algorithm that works on sorted data by repeatedly dividing the search range into halves.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Sorting by repeatedly swapping adjacent elements.
sample_strong_answer: Bubble sort repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order until the list becomes sorted.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Divide and conquer sorting algorithm.
sample_strong_answer: Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that divides an array into smaller parts, sorts them, and merges them back.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Divide and conquer using pivot.
sample_strong_answer: Quick sort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that selects a pivot and partitions elements around it.
follow_up_questions: What is quick sort average complexity?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Function calling itself with base condition.
sample_strong_answer: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller parts of a problem. It must have a base condition to stop.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Solving problems by storing overlapping subproblem results.
sample_strong_answer: Dynamic programming is a technique used to solve problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid repeated work.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: High-level interpreted language and common uses.
sample_strong_answer: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language known for simple syntax and wide use in web development, data analysis, automation, and AI.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Ordered mutable collection.
sample_strong_answer: A list in Python is an ordered and mutable collection that can store multiple items, including different data types.
follow_up_questions: How do you add elements to a list?

question_text: What is a tuple in Python?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Ordered immutable collection.
sample_strong_answer: A tuple is an ordered collection in Python that cannot be changed after creation.

question_text: What is a dictionary in Python?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Key-value pair collection.
sample_strong_answer: A dictionary in Python stores data in key-value pairs. It is useful for fast lookup using keys.
follow_up_questions: How do you access dictionary values?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Reusable block of code.
sample_strong_answer: A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. It helps reduce repetition and improves code organization.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Named storage for data.
sample_strong_answer: A variable is a named memory location used to store data values that can be used and changed in a program.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Classification of values.
sample_strong_answer: Data types define the type of value a variable can store, such as integer, float, string, boolean, list, or dictionary.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Object-oriented platform-independent language.
sample_strong_answer: Java is an object-oriented programming language known for platform independence because Java code runs on the JVM.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Java Virtual Machine that runs bytecode.
sample_strong_answer: JVM stands for Java Virtual Machine. It executes Java bytecode and helps Java achieve platform independence.

question_text: Difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM.
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Java development kit, runtime environment, and virtual machine.
sample_strong_answer: JDK is used to develop Java programs, JRE provides runtime environment, and JVM executes Java bytecode.
follow_up_questions: Which one is needed to run Java programs?
Which one is needed to develop Java programs?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Version control system.
sample_strong_answer: Git is a distributed version control system used to track changes in code and collaborate with others.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Cloud platform for hosting Git repositories.
sample_strong_answer: GitHub is an online platform used to host Git repositories, collaborate on projects, and manage code.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Software Development Life Cycle phases.
sample_strong_answer: SDLC stands for Software Development Life Cycle. It is a process followed to design, develop, test, deploy, and maintain software.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Finding and fixing errors in code.
sample_strong_answer: Debugging is the process of finding, analyzing, and fixing errors or bugs in a program.

question_text: What is testing in software development?
what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Checking software quality and correctness.
sample_strong_answer: Testing is the process of checking whether software works as expected and meets requirements.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Testing individual components/functions.
sample_strong_answer: Unit testing is testing small individual parts of a program, such as functions or methods, to ensure they work correctly.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Interface for software communication.
sample_strong_answer: API stands for Application Programming Interface. It allows two software applications to communicate with each other.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Web API style using HTTP methods.
sample_strong_answer: A REST API is an API that follows REST principles and uses HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE to access resources.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Lightweight data exchange format.
sample_strong_answer: JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation. It is a lightweight format used to store and exchange data in key-value structure.
follow_up_questions: Why is JSON used in APIs?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Protecting systems and data from attacks.
sample_strong_answer: Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting computers, networks, applications, and data from unauthorized access or attacks.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Verifying user identity.
sample_strong_answer: Authentication is the process of verifying who a user is, usually using username-password, OTP, or biometrics.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Checking user permissions.
sample_strong_answer: Authorization is the process of checking what actions or resources an authenticated user is allowed to access.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Converting readable data into unreadable form.
sample_strong_answer: Encryption is the process of converting readable data into unreadable form to protect it from unauthorized access.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: One-way conversion of data into fixed-length value.
sample_strong_answer: Hashing converts data into a fixed-length hash value. It is commonly used for password storage and data integrity checks.
follow_up_questions: Why is hashing used for passwords?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Using computing resources over internet.
sample_strong_answer: Cloud computing means using servers, storage, databases, and software over the internet instead of only local machines.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: System that provides services/resources to clients.
sample_strong_answer: A server is a computer or program that provides services, data, or resources to other computers called clients.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Clients request services and servers respond.
sample_strong_answer: Client-server architecture is a model where clients send requests and servers process those requests and send responses.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Translates entire source code to machine code.
sample_strong_answer: A compiler translates source code into machine code or intermediate code before execution.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Executes code line by line.
sample_strong_answer: An interpreter translates and executes code line by line during runtime.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Integrated Development Environment for coding.
sample_strong_answer: An IDE is software that provides tools like code editor, compiler/interpreter support, debugger, and project management for development.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Step-by-step procedure to solve a problem.
sample_strong_answer: An algorithm is a step-by-step set of instructions used to solve a specific problem.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Informal code-like representation of logic.
sample_strong_answer: Pseudocode is a simple, informal way to write program logic using plain language and programming-like structure.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Diagrammatic representation of steps.
sample_strong_answer: A flowchart is a diagram that represents steps of a process or algorithm using symbols and arrows.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Tracking and managing changes in files/code.
sample_strong_answer: Version control is a system used to track changes in files and code, allowing developers to manage versions and collaborate.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Extra storage and slower write operations.
sample_strong_answer: Indexes improve read speed but require extra storage and can slow down insert, update, and delete operations because indexes also need updating.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Attack by inserting malicious SQL input.
sample_strong_answer: SQL injection is a security attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into input fields to access or modify database data.
follow_up_questions: How can SQL injection be prevented?

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Parameterized query preventing SQL injection.
sample_strong_answer: A prepared statement is a precompiled SQL query that uses parameters instead of directly inserting user input, helping prevent SQL injection.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Model View Controller design pattern.
sample_strong_answer: MVC stands for Model View Controller. It separates application logic into data model, user interface view, and controller handling user input.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Iterative software development approach.
sample_strong_answer: Agile is an iterative software development methodology where teams build software in small increments and adapt based on feedback.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Sequential SDLC model.
sample_strong_answer: Waterfall is a sequential software development model where each phase is completed before moving to the next phase.

what_interviewer_is_looking_for: Understanding what users/business need.
sample_strong_answer: Requirement analysis is the process of collecting and understanding what the software should do based on user or business needs.
follow_up_questions: What happens if requirements are unclear?
